---
title: Calibrating selection of comparative biogeographic models with hyperpriors
authors:
  - name: João Fentanes Machado
    affiliation:
      - id: fsp
        name: Universidade Federal de São Paulo
        city: São Paulo
        country: Brazil
  - name: Jamie Oaks
    orcid: 0000-0002-3757-3836
    url: https://phyletica.org
    corresponding: true
    email: joaks@auburn.edu
    affiliation:
      - id: aumnh
        name: Auburn University Museum of Natural History
        city: Auburn
        state: AL
        country: USA
        url: https://aumnh.auburn.edu/
      - id: audbs
        name: Auburn University Department of Biological Sciences
        city: Auburn
        state: AL
        country: USA
        url: https://www.auburn.edu/cosam/departments/biology/index.htm
abstract: |
  The abstract goes here.
keywords:
  - biogeography
  - comparative phylogeography
  - Bayesian model choise
  - Bayesian hyperprior
number-sections: true
---

## Introduction
Reconstructing the timing of evolutionary events is fundamental for understanding key processes in comparative phylogeography,
including the mechanisms driving lineage diversification, the emergence of biogeographic patterns, and the assembly of biodiversity
across regional landscapes. Divergence-time estimates provide a temporal framework for reconstructing the evolutionary history of populations
and species, allowing researchers to investigate how geological, climatic, and ecological processes have shaped biodiversity patterns and
whether different lineages have responded synchronously to shared historical events. Bayesian approaches have become widely used for estimating
divergence times because they allow the integration of prior knowledge while explicitly accounting for uncertainty in evolutionary parameters.
Within this framework

Such a Bayesian approach has been used with
simulation-based [@Hickerson2006;@Huang2011]
and
full-likelihood [@Oaks2018ecoevolity;@Oaks2019codemog]
methods.
It has been shown that these methods can be biased toward over-clustering
(i.e., underestimating the number of divergence times)
[@Oaks2012;@Oaks2014reply].

@Oaks2014reply proposed this tendency to prefer models with too few
divergence-time parameters was caused by the prior probability density placed
on the divergence times.
In Bayesian model choice, the likelihood of a model is averaged over the
priors, making the prior a "penalty" for adding parameters to the model.
Let's cover some basics of Bayesian inference to gain some intuition for why
this is.

## Methods

## Results

### Validating MCMC sampling from prior distribution

If we look at the number of divergence events,
the MCMC samples match the prior distribution
(@fig-prior-hyper-exp-nevents).

![
    Comparing the MCMC sampling of ecoevolity (empirical) against the expected
    prior on the number of divergence events (model). The left and right plots
    compare the cumulative mass and quantiles, respectively, of the MCMC
    samples versus the expected prior. The cumulative density plot includes the
    results of a Kolmogorov-Smirnov test, which was unable to reject that
    ecoevolity sampled from the expected distribution.
](
    ./images/dpp-conc-hypergamma-4-10-time-hyperexp-02-pairs-20-sites-20000-prior-cmf-comparison-nevents.svg
){
    #fig-prior-hyper-exp-nevents
    fig-alt="Comparing the cumulative density of the prior on the number of divergence events vs MCMC sampling"
    fig-align="center"
}

## Discussion

## References

::: {#refs}
:::

# Supporting Information
